schools.summary: Augustine guards the chronology (the leper cleansed in the interval, no contradiction with Matthew); Ambrose: teaching followed at once by the example of works, mercy to a Gentile; Titus: Capernaum for miracles after the teaching.
Eusebius: the centurion, though a Roman officer, judged the miracles no human power and approached Christ as God.

Ellicott: 'dear' = precious in value (Luke less precise than Matthew's 'palsy,' though he inquired); Barnes: valuable and trusted; Lightfoot's parallel of the upright servant Tabi — the master's tender regard for a dying slave.

Clarke/Robertson: the elders sent as intercessors; the Matthew discrepancy resolved — 'what one does through another he does himself,' Luke detailed where Matthew summarizes; Ellicott: 'elders' (no article), the centurion a half-proselyte like Cornelius.

debates.question: How is Luke's embassy of elders reconciled with Matthew's 'the centurion came'?

sides.position: He acted through his agents — Luke detailed, Matthew summary; no contradiction

themes: sent the elders of the Jews

schools.summary: Robertson: 'kept on beseeching,' 'earnestly' (haste, time short); Barnes: they pleaded the centurion 'worthy' for his favour to the Jews — a contrast to his own sense of unworthiness to come.

Gill: a Gentile loving the Jews was rare (enmity common) — likely a proselyte; Lightfoot: building a synagogue at one's own charge a costly act of devotion, the holiness in its dedication to holy use.

Gill: Christ complied freely at once; Robertson: the second embassy (Luke's detail, Matthew merging both); Ellicott: true to nature, the centurion not leaving his dying servant; 'I am not worthy that thou enter under my roof' — deep humility.

themes: Christ's readiness and the centurion's humility

schools.summary: Ellicott: humility stronger than in Matthew — not daring even to approach; Robertson: 'speak with a word,' pais an affectionate term for the slave; Barnes: faith in the healing word (Psalm 107:20) — power at a distance.

themes: not worthy to come unto thee

schools.summary: Gill: 'set under authority' — even an inferior officer commands and is obeyed, arguing from lesser to greater; Christ's authority over disease absolute.
Robertson: the soldier, used to giving and obeying orders, recognized Christ as Master over disease with power to compel obedience — diseases like his soldiers.

Robertson: Christ marvels (Luke's vivid 'turned'), praising the Gentile's faith beyond Israel's; Ellicott: Luke omits the warning of the children of the kingdom cast out (in Matthew) — the emphasis on the praise.

themes: so great faith not in Israel

schools.summary: Gill/Robertson/Ellicott: the messengers found the servant 'whole' (Luke's medical term), healed by Christ's word at a distance.

Clarke: the cure the fruit of the centurion's faith, prayer, and humility — yet these graces themselves the products of God's grace (Titus 2:11); we cannot give ourselves a gracious disposition, but can quench the Spirit.

themes: faith itself the gift of grace

schools.summary: Cyril: Christ comes self-invited here (summoned at Capernaum); Gregory of Nyssa: an ascending order of miracles (sickness → death → Lazarus) leading faith to the resurrection; Titus: the young man certainly dead, answering the cavil.
Bede: Nain near Tabor; the great multitude providentially many witnesses to the miracle.
Gregory of Nyssa: the sum of misery in few words — a widow with no hope of other sons, her only stay lost.
Lightfoot: the burial customs (carried out of the city, many mourners for one known); Barnes on the city gate; the threefold sorrow — dead, only son, widowed mother.

themes: the only son of a widow

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'the Lord' used absolutely (the disciples' reverence, frequent in Luke/John); the miracle springs from tender human sympathy, not a wish to prove his mission; Gill: he knew her case — 'Weep not' the prelude to restoring her son.

Ellicott: the open bier (like Lazarus, John 11:44); the touch halted the bearers; Lightfoot on the Jewish dread of touching the dead, which Christ overrides without defilement, imparting life; the word 'Arise' spoken with divine authority (his own, unlike Elijah's prayer).

Robertson: 'sat up' (Luke's medical term) and 'began to speak' — full proof of life; 'gave him to his mother' a tender touch (Bengel: he had ceased to belong to her); Ellicott: received 'God-given' in a higher sense than at birth.

themes: he that was dead sat up

schools.summary: Gill: not the fear of dread but reverence of the divine majesty; the people glorified God for a great prophet and his visitation (Luke 1:68).
Barnes: awe at one who could raise the dead; the miracle undeniable and public (unplanned meeting, by a word, many witnesses) — a true but partial confession.
Cyril: Luke records this undeniable raising to silence cavils that the centurion's servant was not really dying — a man certainly dead, raised before witnesses.
The fame spread from Galilean Nain through all Judaea and the region, preparing for John's inquiry.
Bede: the disciples report perhaps from envy (cf. John 3:26); Chrysostom: John, imprisoned, seizes the moment when his disciples most need Christ to send them to him.
Cyril: they relate the miracle known to all; the occasion of John's inquiry.

Henry: the doubt was the disciples' (or needing confirmation), not John's settled mind — he sends them to be established, since Christ had not yet openly declared himself; 'the coming One' the Messianic title.

debates.question: Did John himself doubt, or send for his disciples' sake?

sides.position: For the disciples' sake, to confirm their faith (John knew Christ)
A genuine wavering under imprisonment (a minority)

themes: art thou he that should come

schools.summary: Gill: the messengers from John's prison at Machaerus repeat the question faithfully; 'the coming One' (Ellicott).
Cyril of Jerusalem applies it homiletically: John among the righteous dead whom Christ descended to redeem — the herald redeemed by the King.

Ellicott: peculiar to Luke, Christ answers first by works before the messengers' eyes; Clarke (Pearce): Luke's precise vocabulary distinguishes evil spirits from bodily diseases; 'graciously gave sight' — mercy joined to proof.
Barnes: Christ points to the Messianic signs fulfilled (Isaiah 35:5-6; Luke 4:18) — the blind see, the dead raised; Robertson on Luke's specific cures; an answer by works, not a bare claim.
Wesley: 'to the poor the gospel is preached' the greatest mercy and miracle of all — the climax of the Messianic credentials.

themes: tell John what ye have seen
the blind see, the dead raised

schools.summary: Gill: aimed at John's disciples, who doubted and were offended at Christ's lowliness and success; Barnes: the stone of stumbling (Isaiah 8:14; Luke 2:34) — blessed are they who do not stumble at the lowly Messiah.

Cyril: Christ speaks lest any think John (who confessed the Lamb of God) now ignorant or fickle; the Greek expositor: he waits till the disciples leave, lest praise seem flattery; 'a reed shaken' = no fickle waverer, John was steadfast.

themes: what went ye out to see
a reed shaken with the wind

schools.summary: Ellicott: Luke's vivid words for ostentatious luxury and self-indulgence (the Herodian court); the bitter irony of the 'gorgeous robe' later put on Christ (Luke 23:11); John the austere opposite of the soft courtier.

Robertson: the word of God came to John, making him a prophet (a forth-teller of God's message); Barnes: 'the prophet of the Highest' (Luke 1:76); 'more than a prophet' — he pointed out the Messiah present and was himself foretold.

Gill/Wesley: Christ applies Malachi 3:1 to John — the messenger sent before the Lord to prepare his way; Barnes (Luke 1:15-17): the forerunner in Elijah's spirit; John himself the subject of prophecy, and Christ the Lord whose way he prepared.
None born of women greater than John; 'least in the kingdom greater' = a comparison of privilege, not holiness — the humblest believer knows the finished redemption John only heralded.
Cyril of Jerusalem: John greater than Elijah, Enoch, Moses (the Lord declared it); the end of the Old Testament and start of the New.

debates.question: How is the 'least in the kingdom' greater than John?

sides.position: In privilege and knowledge of the finished redemption, not personal holiness

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the publicans received John's baptism where the leaders would not; Ambrose: 'justified God' = confessing sin, men own God just and themselves guilty; Eusebius: believing, they declared God just in all his ways.

Gill: 'the counsel of God' = his revealed command (to repent and be baptized), not his secret purpose; the leaders rejected it; Bede: rejecting grace harms only oneself — 'against themselves,' to their ruin.
Barnes: the mass of Pharisees and lawyers rejected John's call (though some came); the solemn admonition to repent spurned.

Clarke/Robertson: 'And the Lord said' likely a later liturgical addition (the lectionaries began here); its omission shows vv.29-30 are Christ's words; Luke's doubled question sharpens the point, introducing the parable.
Gill: the leaders like froward children who join in no game, rejecting both piping and mourning.
Robertson: the wedding (pipe) and funeral (mourning) games refused; Wesley: no contrivance can please them — the fault in the hearers, not the message.
Gill: John answers the mourning children — austere, fasting, preaching repentance; they slandered his holiness as 'he hath a devil.'
Robertson: Luke alone adds 'bread' and 'wine'; the generation attributed John's abstinence to demonic possession, scorning his austerity.

themes: John came neither eating nor drinking

schools.summary: Gill: Christ came in social freedom, eating with all; Calvin: this rebukes those who measure holiness by outward austerity (else John ranks above the Son of God) — godliness, not abstinence, profits (1 Timothy 4:8).
Robertson: 'friend of sinners' a slur perverted even today; the same unbelief slandering Christ's freedom as it slandered John's austerity.

themes: Son of man eating and drinking

schools.summary: Gill: 'Wisdom' = Christ, the wisdom of God, justified by 'her children' (those given by the Father, born again), who prove his doctrine leads to godliness not license; Luke's 'all' = the universal practice of the wise.

Bede: wisdom justified in deed (Christ eating with the Pharisee and receiving the penitent); Gregory of Nyssa rebukes the separatist self-righteous — Christ the physician visits not to share sin but to impart righteousness.

Gill: distinct from Mary Magdalene and Mary of Bethany, a separate Galilean event; Lightfoot: 'a sinner' = a woman of ill name; Bede on the alabaster (best for preserving ointment) — the notorious sinner comes uninvited in penitent faith.

debates.question: Who was the woman that anointed Jesus here?

sides.position: An unnamed Galilean sinner, distinct from Mary of Bethany's anointing

themes: a woman which was a sinner

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: she stood at his reclining feet, ashamed yet melted with love; washed them with tears, wiped with her hair (her glory abased), kissed and anointed them — lavish penitent devotion.

Barnes: Simon's secret logic — a prophet would know and rebuke a sinner; the Pharisaic view that contact with the guilty defiles; his double error — Christ knew the woman (and Simon's thoughts), and came to save sinners, not spurn them.

Gill: Christ, omniscient, answers Simon's secret thought, proving himself a prophet — 'yea, more than a prophet'; Calvin: he refutes the hidden sentiment, not the spoken word, showing more than human discernment.
Ellicott: 'Master, say on' recognizes Christ as Rabbi; Robertson: a tone of Socratic irony; Simon unsuspectingly invites the parable that will convict him.

themes: Simon, I have somewhat to say

schools.summary: Barnes: the two debts (500 and 50 pence, denarii/day-wages); Clarke: the creditor is God, the debtors Simon (the smaller, having the law) and the woman (the greater) — yet both alike insolvent, sin a debt no creature can pay.

Gill: man has nothing to pay, sin against the infinite a debt no creature can discharge — then the wonderful grace of God the creditor; Barnes: 'frankly forgave' shows the freeness, but God forgives crime as moral governor (with the atonement), not a mere debt.
Robertson/Ellicott: Simon's reluctant, supercilious 'I suppose'; Barnes: he condemned himself, preparing Christ's reproof; Wesley: love follows forgiveness, the unforgiven debtor flees his creditor.

themes: I suppose he to whom he forgave most

schools.summary: Barnes/Clarke: water for the feet a basic rite of hospitality (Genesis 18:4), which Simon omitted (forgetfulness or contempt); Ellicott: his invitation ostentatious patronage, not reverence; the woman washed Christ's feet with tears and hair — love against cold neglect.

Barnes: the kiss a common token of welcome (Genesis 33:4), omitted by Simon; the woman ceaselessly kissed Christ's feet in deepest humility; Clarke: better 'since she came in' — the splendid host lacked what the lowly sinner's love supplied.

themes: ceased not to kiss my feet

schools.summary: Barnes/Clarke: anointing the head with oil a universal Jewish custom (Psalm 23:5), omitted by Simon; the woman gave costly ointment to the feet — the climax of the contrast, little love versus much.

Gill/Lightfoot: love is the fruit and evidence, not the cause, of forgiveness — she was already pardoned (the parable forgives first, love follows); 'for she loved' is evidential ('you may know she is forgiven'), not causal, lest free grace be overthrown.

debates.question: Is the woman's love the cause or the evidence of her forgiveness?

sides.position: The evidence/fruit — she was forgiven first, love follows (the parable's order)
(The causal reading) her love merited pardon

themes: love the fruit not the cause

schools.summary: Barnes: the gracious assurance stills the penitent's anguish, and proves Christ God, for none but God may forgive sin; Ellicott: the perfect tense — forgiveness sealed before, now openly assured for her peace.

Ellicott/Robertson: 'Who even forgiveth sins?' — the same implicit blasphemy charge as Capernaum (Luke 5:21); Barnes: who but God could forgive? — their astonishment pointed to his deity, though they would not own it.

themes: who is this that forgiveth sins

schools.summary: Barnes/Clarke: 'thy faith hath saved thee' — faith the instrument of salvation (Ephesians 2:8), not the love that flowed from it; 'go in peace' both a farewell and the real peace of a forgiven conscience.
